mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Denmark on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — northern Europe, between the North Sea and the Baltic. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Denmark?
mapQuestions.answer: Copenhagen — on the island of Zealand, right across from Sweden.

mapQuestions.question: Which country borders Denmark by land?
mapQuestions.answer: Only Germany — the rest of Denmark is islands.

mapQuestions.question: Roughly how many islands make up Denmark?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 1,400 islands, of which about 70 are inhabited.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Faroe Islands and Greenland's connection to Denmark?
mapQuestions.answer: Both are autonomous countries within the Kingdom of Denmark — they govern themselves but are part of Denmark for defence and foreign policy.

foods.name: Smørrebrød
foods.description: Open-faced sandwich on dense rye bread, topped with anything from pickled herring to roast pork. A Danish lunchtime staple.

foods.name: Wienerbrød
foods.description: What the world calls "Danish pastry". Flaky, buttery, often with custard or jam. Danes don't call them Danishes.

foods.name: Pølser
foods.description: Hot dogs from a street cart (pølsevogn), with crispy fried onions, pickles, and remoulade sauce.

foods.name: Pickled herring
foods.description: Cold pickled fish in vinegar, onion, mustard, or curry. Eaten on rye bread for lunch — yes, fish for lunch.

foods.name: Risalamande
foods.description: Cold rice pudding with cherry sauce. Eaten at Christmas — a whole almond is hidden in the bowl and whoever finds it wins a prize.

foods.name: Frikadeller
foods.description: Danish meatballs — flatter than Swedish ones, usually fried, served with potatoes and gravy.

scavengerItems.label: A castle (Denmark has loads — Hamlet's castle Kronborg is real)
A wind turbine (Denmark gets 50%+ of electricity from wind)
Brass duck-and-ducklings statues crossing a road (a Copenhagen thing)
An old wooden sailing ship in a harbour
A LEGO store or a giant LEGO model
A pastry shop window with at least 6 different wienerbrød
Designer Scandinavian furniture in a shop window
A Danish flag flying from someone's birthday party — they do this!
A roller coaster at Tivoli (or Bakken, world's oldest amusement park)

geoMatches.description: The capital. Brightly painted harbour houses, Tivoli Gardens amusement park, royal palaces, and bicycles everywhere.
Denmark's second city, on the Jutland mainland. Lively university town with a famous rainbow-coloured rooftop walkway over the art museum.
Birthplace of fairy-tale writer Hans Christian Andersen ("The Little Mermaid", "The Ugly Duckling"). Charming old town centre.
A small town that became famous because LEGO was invented here in 1932. Home of the original Legoland.
A Danish island in the middle of the Baltic Sea, closer to Sweden and Poland than to mainland Denmark. Famous for sunshine, smokehouses, and round white churches.

senses.smell: Fresh-baked pastries, harbour water, woodsmoke from chimneys, candles in cafés (Danes light SO many candles)…
senses.hear: Bike bells everywhere, church bells, "Hej!" greetings, harbour gulls, café chatter…
senses.taste: Buttery flaky pastry, sharp pickled herring, dark rye bread, salted liquorice (yes, salty!), strong filter coffee…
senses.feel: Cobblestones underfoot, cold harbour wind, soft Scandinavian wool blankets in cafés (hygge!), warm pastry on cold hands…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is "hygge" and why is it such a Danish thing?
stories.body: "Hygge" (HOO-gah) is a Danish word with no exact English translation. It means a feeling of cosiness, warmth, togetherness — soft blankets, candlelight, a hot drink, time spent with people you love, the deliberate slowing down. Denmark has long, dark, cold winters, and Danes have made an art form of being cosy inside while it's miserable outside. Cafés have blankets on the chairs in winter. Homes light dozens of candles. It's part of why Danes are repeatedly named the happiest people in the world.
stories.thinkingQuestion: What's YOUR hygge — the things that make you feel safest and cosiest?

stories.question: How does Denmark have more bicycles than people?
stories.body: Denmark has 5.9 million people and over 6.5 million bicycles. In Copenhagen, more than 50% of people cycle to work or school every day, in any weather. The reason: smart planning. Cities built dedicated bike lanes (often separated from cars by a kerb), traffic lights for bicycles, even bike-only bridges. Bikes have their own paths to schools. Parents take babies in front-mounted boxes (Christiania bikes). Cycling is the fastest way around the city — often faster than cars. The result: less traffic, less pollution, healthier people, and the city is quieter.

stories.question: Where did LEGO come from?
stories.body: In 1932, Ole Kirk Christiansen, an out-of-work Danish carpenter, started making wooden toys in his little workshop in Billund. He named the company LEGO from "Leg Godt" — Danish for "play well". The famous plastic interlocking brick was patented in 1958. Today LEGO is the world's biggest toy company by sales. About 100 billion bricks are made every year. The bricks are so precise that a brick made today still fits perfectly with one from 1958. There are now enough LEGO bricks for every person on Earth to have 80 of them.

stories.question: Is Hamlet's castle real?
stories.body: Yes! Kronborg Castle, on the Danish coast just across from Sweden, is the real-life "Elsinore" castle where Shakespeare set Hamlet. Shakespeare never actually visited (he probably never left England), but his fellow actors did, and they brought back stories of this grand castle perched on the strait between Denmark and Sweden. The Danish royal family used Kronborg to charge a "Sound Toll" — every ship passing through had to pay them — which made Denmark rich for 400 years. Today the castle hosts Hamlet performances every summer, often with Hollywood actors playing the prince.

stories.question: How did tiny Denmark help Jewish people escape the Nazis?
stories.body: When the Nazis occupied Denmark in 1940-45, they began arresting Jewish citizens in 1943. The Danish resistance organised an extraordinary rescue: in a few days in October 1943, around 7,200 Danish Jews — almost the entire Jewish population — were smuggled out of the country across the narrow sea to neutral Sweden, hidden in fishing boats. Danish fishermen, doctors, police, and ordinary citizens all helped. Of all the countries occupied by the Nazis, only Denmark saved nearly all its Jewish citizens. It remains one of the most quietly heroic stories of WWII.
stories.thinkingQuestion: What would you risk to help a stranger you didn't know but who needed your help?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Denmark so far?
Copenhagen bicycle culture — could you do this every day?
Hygge — what's the most hygge moment you've had on this trip?
Denmark consistently tops "happiest country" lists. Why do you think that is?
What's one Danish habit you'd like to bring home?
Denmark has a royal family AND is a democracy. Do you think keeping a royal family is worth it?

wordSearch.hint: Danish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Returning to Denmark after near-extinction in the 1990s — now around 6 nesting pairs, watched carefully.

animals.name: Mute swan
animals.description: Denmark's national bird (Hans Christian Andersen's "Ugly Duckling" was a young swan). On every lake and harbour.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In Jutland forests and Danish national parks. Largest land mammal in the country.

animals.name: Harbour seal
animals.description: Along the North Sea and Wadden Sea coasts — colonies you can visit on boat tours.

animals.name: European hedgehog
animals.description: In gardens and parks, often crossing pavements at dusk. Beloved in Denmark.

animals.name: Harbour porpoise
animals.description: Small dolphin species in the Belt Sea — sometimes glimpsed from ferries.

animals.name: White-tailed eagle
animals.description: Recovered from near-extinction — about 200 pairs now nest in Denmark.
